agents.risk: The continued high unemployment rate and very low income levels may prevent the new housing supply from being affordable for large families like mine, exacerbating the existing housing crisis.
agents.who_bears_it: Families with very low income and large household sizes who are renters.
agents.cascade: High unemployment leads to low income, increasing vulnerability to housing affordability issues despite new home construction.

agents.risk: The ongoing housing affordability issues for very low-income single renters may be exacerbated by the increase in competition from newly constructed homes, which may not be affordable to those earning
agents.who_bears_it: Single renters aged 18-24 with very low income in Reserve Northern Ontario.
agents.cascade: The potential increase in property values due to new home construction could lead to higher rent prices, compounding existing affordability challenges for low-income individuals.

agents.risk: The construction of new homes may not lower rent prices quickly enough for low-income renters in Nunavut, exacerbating the existing housing affordability crisis.
agents.who_bears_it: Low-income renters aged 25-34 in Nunavut who struggle with high rents and low wages.
agents.cascade: High unemployment and low income levels hinder the affordability of new homes, compounding existing housing challenges.

agents.risk: The construction of new homes may lead to increased competition and higher property values, further complicating housing affordability for medium-income couples in Nunavut despite low debt levels.
agents.who_bears_it: Medium-income couples, particularly those aged 50-64, who are already facing high rental prices and
agents.cascade: New homes may not alleviate the housing crisis if they are unaffordable, leading to stagnant wages and increased financial strain on existing residents.

risks.title: Exacerbation of Housing Affordability Crisis
risks.description: The construction of 500,000 new homes could paradoxically worsen housing affordability due to increased competition and subsequent property value increases. This effect is observed across various demographic groups including both renters and owners in cities such as Toronto, Edmonton, and Montreal.
risks.affected_groups: Medium-income families, high-income renters, low-income households
risks.cascade_effect: Higher property values could lead to elevated rent prices, compounding financial strain for all renters.

risks.title: Limited Impact on Rent Prices for Low-Income Renters
risks.description: Despite the influx of new homes, low-income renters in areas like Nunavut may not see a decrease in rent prices quickly enough, maintaining their vulnerability within the housing market.
risks.cascade_effect: The new homes' insufficient impact on rent could interact with high unemployment to deepen the housing crisis.

risks.title: Increased Financial Strain Due to Rising Property Values
risks.description: Increased property values could further limit affordability for medium-income families, leading to a potential rise in debt levels as these households struggle to maintain their living standards.
risks.cascade_effect: Higher debt levels could result from rising property values, intensifying the existing affordability issues.

risk_report.blind_spots: Insights into the impacts on indigenous communities and single-parent households are limited.
risk_report.key_insight: Increased competition from new homes may raise property values, which in turn exacerbates existing affordability issues across diverse demographic groups.

confidence.reason: strong StatsCan data coverage for this policy type; national scope matches agent distribution well; all agents responded successfully
confidence.caveat: This is a hypothesis generation tool. Findings should be validated against real survey data before informing policy decisions.
